1:
“Then Pilate therefore took Jesus, and scourged him.” The witnesses read this as Pilate’s attempt to satisfy the Jews with a punishment short of death. The grammatical-historical commentators note that, finding the people would not have Jesus released but Barabbas, Pilate, “very desirous to save his life” (Gill), resorted to scourging — Robertson observing the causative sense, “Pilate did not actually scourge Jesus,” but “ordered it done, perhaps to see if the mob would relent” (cf. Matthew 27:26; Isaiah 53:5). The Latin Augustine reads the same motive: Pilate “acted thus for no other reason than that the Jews, glutted with the outpoured blood of Jesus… might cool down to a milder mood.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, sees Pilate adding “to the former ignominy a second,” hoping that “when Christ shall have been scourged, the Jews will be satisfied with this light chastisement.” But where Pilate’s expedient fails, Calvin bids us “recognize the decree of Heaven, by which Christ was appointed” to bear this suffering. The witnesses thus find in the verse the beginning of the Passion’s outward agonies, and the irony of Pilate’s plan: a judge who has twice declared Jesus innocent yet scourges him, hoping to placate his accusers with cruelty short of death. The scourging, the witnesses note, was no light thing but a brutal Roman punishment, fulfilling the prophet’s word, “with his stripes we are healed” (Isaiah 53:5); and Pilate’s attempt to use it as a compromise only entangles him deeper in the guilt of condemning the guiltless. The decree of God overrules the weakness of the judge, that the Son might be scourged for his people.

The witnesses note the moral collapse the verse records. Pilate, the appointed guardian of justice, uses a cruel punishment not to vindicate the innocent but to bargain with the guilty. Scourging was meant for the condemned; to inflict it on one declared faultless is to make injustice a tool of policy. The witnesses find here a warning of how the fear of men corrupts a judge: Pilate, knowing Christ innocent, hopes that a measure of suffering will buy him peace with the mob, and so takes the first fatal step toward the very sentence he means to avoid.
2:
“And the soldiers platted a crown of thorns, and put it on his head, and they put on him a purple robe.” The witnesses dwell on the mock-royal attire and its deeper meaning. The grammatical-historical commentators see the soldiers dressing Jesus as a sham king — the purple robe of royalty and a crown, but of thorns (Clarke; cf. Matthew 27:29). Lightfoot finds in the thorns “a most unquestionable token that Christ’s kingdom was not of this world,” crowned “with thorns and briers, which were the curse of this earth” (Genesis 3:18). Gill reads the thorns as “an emblem of his being surrounded by wicked men… and of the sins of his people compassing him about,” grievous to him as he suffered.

Calvin, for the Reformed, notes that this was done “by the authority of Pilate, in order to affix a mark of infamy on the Son of God,” to satisfy the rage of the Jews; yet “the wickedness and insolence of the soldiers” was overruled by God. The witnesses thus find in the crown of thorns a scene of deepest irony and meaning. The soldiers meant only to mock a would-be king; but unwittingly they proclaimed a truth — that the King of kings wears, in this world, a crown of the curse, taking upon his own brow the thorns that were the sign of Adam’s fall. The purple robe and the thorny crown, meant for derision, become emblems of his true royalty and of his bearing the curse for his people. What the soldiers did in scorn, the witnesses read as a kind of prophecy: the suffering Saviour, crowned with the curse he came to remove, reigns from the very shame heaped upon him.

The witnesses also dwell on the patience of the Sufferer. He who could have summoned legions submits to the soldiers’ rough hands, letting them press the thorns upon his brow and drape the purple about his torn shoulders. In this silent endurance the witnesses see both the reality of his manhood, which truly felt the pain and shame, and the majesty of his love, which bore them willingly. The mock coronation thus becomes, to the eye of faith, the enthroning of the King of love, who wins his crown not by conquest but by suffering.
3:
“And said, Hail, King of the Jews! and they smote him with their hands.” The witnesses picture the soldiers’ cruel mockery of Christ’s kingship. The grammatical-historical commentators note the mock homage: Robertson reads the imperfects as repeated action — “they kept coming and saying” in “derision and mock reverence,” with “Hail!” (Ave) as if to Caesar, and “the King” in address — while they “struck him with their hands” again and again. Gill describes them prostrating themselves and bowing the knee “in a mock way, as if he was an earthly monarch,” then buffeting him. The blows and the salutation together make a bitter parody of a king’s court, the soldiers paying false homage with one breath and striking with the next.

The witnesses dwell on the patience of Christ under this usage. He who is indeed the King of the Jews — and of all — receives the mock crown, the mock robe, the mock salute, and the blows, without resistance or complaint. The witnesses find here the depth of his humiliation: the eternal Son, before whom angels veil their faces, stands as the sport of a Roman guard, hailed in scorn as a king and beaten as a fool. Yet the very mockery, the witnesses note, proclaims the truth it means to deny: he is the King of the Jews, and the soldiers’ derisive “Hail” is, despite itself, a confession. The patience with which he bears the shame is itself royal — the dignity of a King who reigns not by force but by suffering love, enduring the cross and despising the shame (Hebrews 12:2). In the mock court of Pilate’s soldiers, the true King is most truly revealed.

The witnesses draw the lesson that the world’s scorn cannot touch the true honour of Christ. The soldiers meant their salute as an insult; yet every knee shall one day bow to the One they mocked. The verse thus sets the present shame against the coming glory: the King hailed in derision and struck with the open hand is the same who will return in the glory of the Father. And his bearing under the blows teaches his people how to suffer wrong — not rendering railing for railing, but committing themselves to him who judges righteously.
4:
“Pilate therefore went forth again, and saith unto them, Behold, I bring him forth to you, that ye may know that I find no fault in him.” The witnesses note Pilate’s renewed declaration of Christ’s innocence even as he produces him scourged. The grammatical-historical commentators recall that Pilate had already gone forth and declared “I find no fault in him” (John 18:38), and now, having scourged Jesus to appease the mob, brings him out again with the same verdict (Barnes). Robertson pictures the scene vividly — Pilate “leading Jesus out of the palace before the mob” — and reads the purpose clause, “that ye may come to know,” by this mockery, the sincerity of Pilate’s judgment of innocence. The strange contradiction is plain: Pilate punishes one he pronounces guiltless, hoping the sight of a scourged and humbled prisoner will move the people to relent.

Cyril of Jerusalem, looking back from the Eastern church, bids believers “not be ashamed of the Cross of our Saviour, but rather glory in it,” for “the word of the Cross is unto Jews a stumbling-block, and unto Gentiles foolishness, but to us salvation.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the deepening of Pilate’s guilt and the irony of his position. He testifies repeatedly to Christ’s innocence — “I find no fault in him” — and yet, instead of releasing him, he scourges him and parades him before his accusers, hoping pity will accomplish what justice should have done. The witnesses note that Pilate’s threefold avowal of innocence (here, and in John 18:38, and again to come) only condemns the judge: knowing Jesus guiltless, he yet yields him to suffering and death. The repeated “no fault” stands as the world’s own testimony that the Lamb of God was without blemish, condemned not for any fault of his own but for the sins of others.

The witnesses observe the cowardice masked as fairness. Pilate will say the right thing — ‘I find no fault’ — but will not do the right thing and release the prisoner. His repeated avowal becomes, against himself, the clearest proof of Christ’s innocence on the lips of the very man who sends him to the cross. The verse thus exposes the futility of half-measures in the face of evil: Pilate hopes to satisfy both conscience and crowd, and ends by betraying conscience to the crowd, condemning the One he cannot but declare guiltless.
5:
“Then came Jesus forth, wearing the crown of thorns, and the purple robe. And Pilate saith unto them, Behold the man!” The witnesses dwell on this most famous of Pilate’s words. The grammatical-historical commentators read the scene: Jesus comes forth bearing the marks of scourging, the thorny crown, and the purple robe, and Pilate points to him — Barnes judges “his object evidently was to move them to compassion, and to convince them, by a sight of the Saviour himself, that he was innocent.” Clarke paraphrases the taunt: “Behold the man who, according to you, affects the government… Behold the man whom ye have brought to me as an enemy of Caesar — is he not beneath contempt?” Robertson notes the present participle “wearing” and that “Jesus bore the mockery with kingly dignity as part of the shame of the Cross” (Hebrews 12:2); the exclamation “Behold the man” (Ecce Homo) is Pilate’s.

The witnesses find in the words, beyond Pilate’s intention, a depth he never meant. Pilate said “Behold the man” to provoke pity or contempt — see this poor, beaten figure, no king to fear. But the church has heard in the words a far greater meaning: behold the Man indeed, the true and perfect Man, the second Adam, standing in shame for the sins of the race; behold the Man of Sorrows, acquainted with grief; behold the One in whom alone humanity is seen as God meant it to be, never more truly man than in the patience of his suffering. The witnesses thus read the verse as both the lowest point of Christ’s public humiliation and, unwittingly, a proclamation. Pilate meant to display a broken prisoner; he displayed the Saviour of the world, crowned with thorns, robed in mock purple, bearing in his marred face the love that bore the cross. “Behold the man” — and in him, behold the salvation of God.

The witnesses note the strange providence by which the heathen judge becomes an unwitting herald. Pilate points to a beaten prisoner; the church beholds her Lord. The very figure meant to excite the crowd’s contempt has become, through the centuries, the object of the world’s adoration — the thorn-crowned Man whose marred visage draws more love than any earthly majesty. So the verse teaches that the deepest revelation of God is given in the lowest abasement of Christ: it is precisely as the suffering, silent, wounded Man that he is most truly to be beheld and worshipped.
6:
“When the chief priests therefore and officers saw him, they cried out, saying, Crucify him, crucify him. Pilate saith unto them, Take ye him, and crucify him: for I find no fault in him.” The witnesses note that the pitiable sight, far from softening the rulers, inflames them. The grammatical-historical commentators observe that “the view of the Saviour’s meekness only exasperated them the more” (Barnes); having resolved on his death, they redouble their cry, “Crucify him, crucify him” (Robertson notes the urgent aorist imperative; cf. Matthew 27:22). Pilate’s retort — “Take ye him, and crucify him” — is, the commentators agree, not a real permission but a bitter, ironic refusal: Calvin says “he did not wish to deliver Christ into their hands… only he declares that he will not be their executioner,” for he “will never be persuaded to shed innocent blood for their gratification,” repeating yet again, “I find no fault in him.”

The Latin Augustine marks how “the envy of the Jews does not subside at Christ’s disgraces, but rather rises”; the Eastern Chrysostom reads Pilate’s words as “the speech of a man abhorring” the deed they demand. The witnesses thus find in the verse the collision of a relentless hatred and a weak, divided judge. The rulers, unmoved by innocence or suffering, will have nothing but crucifixion; Pilate, convinced of innocence yet unwilling to act on it, throws the deed back upon them with a sneer that is really a confession of his own helplessness. His third declaration of “no fault” only deepens the irony: the Roman governor, the representative of law, three times pronounces the prisoner guiltless and three times fails to free him. The witnesses note the tragedy of Pilate — knowing the right, declaring it, yet lacking the courage to do it — and the awful resolve of the rulers, whom no sight of meekness or innocence can turn from their purpose to crucify the Lord of glory.

The witnesses draw the sober lesson of Pilate’s ruin. He had every advantage — the authority to release, the conviction of innocence, the warnings of conscience — yet he lacked the one thing needful, the courage to act on the truth he knew. The verse thus stands as a perpetual warning against the sin of knowing better and doing worse. And it magnifies, by contrast, the resolve of divine love: where men’s hatred drives them to crucify and men’s weakness lets them, the purpose of God to redeem moves steadily on, using even the malice of the rulers and the cowardice of the judge to accomplish salvation.
7:
“The Jews answered him, We have a law, and by our law he ought to die, because he made himself the Son of God.” The witnesses note that here, at last, the rulers name the real ground of their hostility. The grammatical-historical commentators observe the shift of charge: having failed to make sedition stick before Pilate (Luke 23:2), they fall back on the accusation of blasphemy (Gill), appealing to “the law respecting blasphemy” (Barnes; Leviticus 24:16; cf. Deuteronomy 18:20). Ellicott prefers the reading “by the law he ought to die,” the rulers stung by Pilate’s sarcasm and appealing to their own law. Robertson marks the decisive point: “here at last the Sanhedrin give the real ground for their hostility… he made himself the Son of God,” the charge of long standing (John 5:18) on which they had voted his condemnation (Mark 14:61–64).

Calvin, for the Reformed, notes the rulers’ pretence of righteousness: “they mean that, in proceeding against Christ, they do what is right, and are not actuated by hatred or sinful passion,” feeling that Pilate “had indirectly reproved them.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the unveiling of the true issue. The charge of sedition was a pretext for Pilate’s ears; the real offence, in the rulers’ own eyes, was that Jesus “made himself the Son of God.” The accusation is, from their standpoint, blasphemy worthy of death; but it is, in truth, the very confession of who he is. The witnesses note the irony: the Jews would put him to death for claiming to be the Son of God, the one claim that, if true — and it is true — makes his death the redemption of the world. The deepest reason for their hatred is the deepest truth of the gospel; they condemn him for being what he is, and in condemning him fulfil the purpose for which the Son of God came.

The witnesses note how the verse changes the whole complexion of the trial. Before Pilate the charge had been political — a rival to Caesar; but pressed by the governor’s sarcasm, the rulers blurt out the real grievance, which is theological. They cannot bear that this man calls God his own Father and makes himself equal with God. The witnesses thus find the cross planted, in the end, on the rock of Christ’s deity: he dies, in his accusers’ own account, for being the Son of God — the truth they reject as blasphemy and the church confesses as the ground of her salvation.
8:
“When Pilate therefore heard that saying, he was the more afraid.” The witnesses dwell on the new fear that gripped the governor at the charge that Jesus “made himself the Son of God.” The grammatical-historical commentators note that this accusation, which Pilate “had not before heard” in this form, convinced him “more of their malignity and wickedness” (Barnes), and stirred a deeper dread. Robertson reads it as superstitious fear — “the claim of Jesus to deity excited Pilate’s superstitious fears,” the more since his wife had already warned him (Matthew 27:19). Clarke explains the legal turn: while the charge was mere sedition, Pilate “knew he could deliver him,” but a capital charge “from their own laws” changed his footing. Ellicott reads the fear as wonder “who Jesus really was,” having himself heard him claim “a kingdom which is not of this world.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, allows two senses: that Pilate “dreaded lest some blame should be imputed to him if a tumult arose,” or, more likely, that “after having heard the name of the Son of God, his mind was moved by religion.” Wesley notes that he “seems to have been afraid before of shedding innocent blood,” and now the more. The witnesses thus find in the verse the working of conscience and superstition together in a pagan judge. The very claim that the rulers urged as ground for death — that this man is the Son of God — strikes Pilate not with scorn but with fear. Half-pagan, half-awakened, he senses that he may have to do with no ordinary prisoner, and the dread of it unsettles him. The witnesses note the irony: the title that the Jews count blasphemy fills the Gentile with awe, so that the truth of Christ’s deity, rejected by his own, begins to terrify the heathen who must judge him.

The witnesses observe how the truth presses even on an unwilling heart. Pilate would gladly have kept the matter on the safe ground of Roman law and Galilean politics; but the word ‘Son of God’ opens a door he cannot close, raising the dread that he may be trifling with heaven. The verse thus shows that the claim of Christ’s deity is no idle thing: it disturbs even the careless, and lays on every hearer the question Pilate could not escape — who, then, is this man?
9:
“And went again into the judgment hall, and saith unto Jesus, Whence art thou? But Jesus gave him no answer.” The witnesses dwell on Pilate’s anxious question and Christ’s deliberate silence. The grammatical-historical commentators explain that “Whence art thou?” is no inquiry about his country — Pilate knew he was a Galilean (Luke 23:6) — but about his nature: whether “a mere man, or whether divine” (Barnes), “a man born on the earth, or… some god” (Calvin). Robertson reads Pilate as “really alarmed.” On the silence, the commentators note its dignity and meaning: Jesus had already told Pilate of his kingdom and his witness to the truth (John 18:36–37), and to one who had heard and would not act, no further answer was owed (Chrysostom).

Augustine, for the Latin fathers, gathers the Gospel accounts: this silence “was maintained more than once — before the High Priest, before Herod, and before Pilate,” so that the prophecy was “amply fulfilled,” “As a sheep before her shearers is dumb, so opened he not his mouth” (Isaiah 53:7). The Eastern Chrysostom notes that Pilate, having heard Christ’s words, “ought to have resisted and rescued Him,” and forfeited the right to more. The witnesses thus find in the verse the majestic reticence of the Lamb. Christ, who had spoken freely to teach and to testify, is silent now before a judge who asks not to learn but to escape his own duty. The silence is not sullenness but judgment: there is a point past which the trifler with truth receives no more light. And it fulfils the prophet’s picture of the suffering Servant — dumb before his shearers, opening not his mouth — so that even Christ’s silence preaches, declaring him the foretold Lamb led to the slaughter for the sins of his people.

The witnesses draw the warning hidden in Christ’s silence. To the seeking soul Christ speaks freely; but to the one who hears the truth and will not obey it, there comes at last a silence. Pilate had been told enough; further words would only have been wasted on a will already bending to the crowd. The verse thus teaches that light refused is light withdrawn, and that the dumbness of the Lamb before his judge is itself a solemn judgment on those who ask after truth with no purpose to follow it.
10:
“Then saith Pilate unto him, Speakest thou not unto me? knowest thou not that I have power to crucify thee, and have power to release thee?” The witnesses hear in this the voice of wounded pride and pagan presumption. The grammatical-historical commentators note the emphatic position of the pronoun: “To me dost thou not speak?” (Ellicott) — Pilate “was not accustomed to be met with silence like this” (Barnes), and resented it as contempt of court (Robertson). He tries to “appall him with the declaration that his life was at his disposal,” boasting of a twofold “power” (exousia, better “authority”) — to crucify or to release. Ellicott catches his wavering: he “now as man trembles before One who may be a Being from the other world, and now as Roman governor expects that Being to tremble before him.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, marks how quickly the dread of v.8 evaporates: “the dread with which Pilate had been suddenly seized was transitory, and had no solid root; for now, forgetting all fear, he breaks out into haughty and monstrous contempt of God.” He “threatens Christ, as if there had not been a Judge in heaven” — the way of “irreligious men,” who shake off conviction as soon as it passes. The witnesses thus find in the verse the instability of a conscience without faith. A moment ago Pilate trembled at the thought that he might be judging the Son of God; now, stung by silence, he asserts his own authority over life and death as though he were absolute. The boast is hollow, as the next verse will show; but it reveals the man — proud, frightened, and fickle by turns, grasping at his Roman power to cover the fear he cannot master. The witnesses note that Christ’s silence has done its work, drawing out the pride that the answer of v.11 will rebuke.

The witnesses note the emptiness of the boast. Pilate claims a power he is, at that very moment, unable to use — for though he has authority to release, the fear of the crowd has already bound his hands. The verse thus exposes the hollowness of all merely human power when it is severed from the courage to do right: Pilate has the office but not the freedom, the title of judge but not the will to judge justly. His proud words ring loud precisely because his real authority has already failed him.
11:
“Jesus answered, Thou couldest have no power at all against me, except it were given thee from above: therefore he that delivered me unto thee hath the greater sin.” The witnesses dwell on Christ’s rebuke of Pilate’s boast and his weighing of the greater guilt. The grammatical-historical commentators stress the doctrine of providence: Pilate “had of himself neither power of life nor power of death,” but only “that which was given to him from above” (Ellicott); Barnes — “God has ordered your life, your circumstances, and the extent of your dominion.” Robertson reads “from above” (anōthen) of God (cf. John 3:3; Romans 13:1), the doctrine of ordained government. On the “greater sin,” the commentators identify the one who “delivered” him: Wesley names Caiaphas, “knowing this, more blamable than thou”; Clarke includes “the Jews who delivered me to thee, and Judas who delivered me to the Jews,” whose “rage and malice” outweigh Pilate’s ignorance.

Calvin, for the Reformed, reads the saying both of God’s overruling providence — “Pilate, though he thinks that he can do all things, will do nothing more than God permits” — and of the relative guilt of the actors. The witnesses thus find in the verse two great truths held together. The first is the sovereignty of God over all earthly power: Pilate’s authority is no possession of his own but a trust “given from above,” so that even in condemning Christ he does only what God permits, and the cross is no triumph of Roman might but the working of the divine purpose. The second is the reality of human guilt within that providence: that an act is permitted by God does not excuse those who do it. Pilate sins in condemning the innocent; but those who, against fuller light and out of settled malice, delivered Christ to him — the high priest and the nation — bear “the greater sin.” The witnesses note how Christ, even as prisoner, remains the Judge, measuring the guilt of his judges and affirming, in the face of Pilate’s boast, the throne of God above all earthly thrones.
12:
“And from thenceforth Pilate sought to release him: but the Jews cried out, saying, If thou let this man go, thou art not Caesar’s friend: whosoever maketh himself a king speaketh against Caesar.” The witnesses mark the turning point at which the rulers’ threat overcomes the governor’s resolve. The grammatical-historical commentators note that Christ’s words moved Pilate the more to free him: Robertson reads the imperfect “kept on seeking” to release, “renewed efforts,” though “afraid to act boldly against the will of the Jews.” But the cry “Thou art not Caesar’s friend” strikes home — Clarke counts this among Pilate’s five attempts to release Jesus (cf. Luke 23:4, 23:22), all defeated here; Ellicott and Robertson read the words as “a direct threat,” for Pilate “knew all the time that the Sanhedrin might tell Caesar on him.” To be denounced to a jealous emperor as harbouring a rival king was a danger Pilate dared not face.

Augustine, for the Latin fathers, notes the shrewdness of the rulers: “the Jews thought they could alarm Pilate more by the mention of Caesar than by telling him of their law.” Calvin, for the Reformed, judges Pilate “wretchedly irresolute,” “actuated more by ambition than by a regard to justice.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the decisive collapse of Pilate’s will. Convinced of Christ’s innocence and newly sobered by his words, he genuinely sought to release him; but the moment his own standing with Caesar is threatened, his resolve breaks. The fear of man, the witnesses note, proves stronger in him than the fear of God or the voice of justice: he will not risk his office and perhaps his life for the sake of a guiltless prisoner. The cry of the rulers is a calculated snare, playing on the one fear Pilate cannot master; and by it they drag the reluctant judge from seeking Christ’s release to pronouncing his death. The world’s hatred, armed with political menace, overpowers a conscience that has truth but not courage.

The witnesses draw the tragic lesson of a conscience overruled by self-interest. Pilate knew the right and even desired it; what he lacked was the willingness to suffer for it. The moment his own safety was set against justice, justice lost. The verse thus stands as a perpetual study of the cost of discipleship reversed: the man who will not risk his place for the truth will betray the truth to keep his place. And it shows the malice of the rulers at its most cunning, wielding loyalty to Caesar as the lever to move a faltering judge.
13:
“When Pilate therefore heard that saying, he brought Jesus forth, and sat down in the judgment seat in a place that is called the Pavement, but in the Hebrew, Gabbatha.” The witnesses mark the solemn moment when Pilate takes his seat to pronounce sentence. The grammatical-historical commentators describe the scene: Robertson notes “he took his seat upon the bēma,” the raised platform of judgment (cf. Acts 25:6), “the examination over” and the final stage at hand. The place, “the Pavement” (Lithostrōton, a stone pavement of coloured stones, like that of Ahasuerus, Esther 1:6), was called in Hebrew “Gabbatha” — Wesley takes it of “the high place,” an eminence where the judge could be seen. Lightfoot wonders whether it names the hall Gazith where the Sanhedrin sat. Ellicott notes Pilate “hesitates no longer”: his own position threatened, he prepares to pronounce the sentence.

Calvin, for the Reformed, sees the inner conflict still: “what conflicting opinions passed through the mind of Pilate, as if he had been a stage-player acting two characters” — he “ascends to pronounce sentence of death on Christ solemnly,” yet “declares openly that he does so reluctantly.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the formal beginning of the end. Pilate, his resistance broken by the threat of Caesar, mounts the judgment seat to do what he knows to be unjust. The trappings of legal solemnity — the tribunal, the Pavement, the official seat — only deepen the irony: all the forms of justice are observed in the doing of a great injustice. The witnesses note the providential undertone as well, for the very seat of human judgment becomes the place where the Judge of all is judged. As Pilate sits to sentence Christ, the deeper truth stands behind the scene — that the One arraigned will one day sit upon a greater throne, before which Pilate and all his judges must appear.

The witnesses note the gathering of providence behind the human drama. Every detail — the seat, the Pavement, the Hebrew name — is recorded by an eyewitness who stood near, and every detail serves a purpose beyond Pilate’s knowing. The judge who mounts the tribunal to condemn the innocent is himself being judged by the scene he sets; and the reader, knowing the end, sees in the solemn forms of Roman justice the unfolding of a sentence decreed in heaven, by which the Son of God should be delivered up for the salvation of the world.
14:
“And it was the preparation of the passover, and about the sixth hour: and he saith unto the Jews, Behold your King!” The witnesses attend both to the disputed hour and to Pilate’s bitter taunt. The grammatical-historical commentators identify “the preparation of the passover” as the Friday of passover week (Robertson; cf. Mark 15:42), and grapple with the famous chronological difficulty: John’s “about the sixth hour” seems to clash with Mark’s “third hour” (Mark 15:25). Ellicott (in this context) notes the looseness of ancient time-reckoning, the hours being rough divisions; Barnes refers the reader to the harmonising discussions; and Calvin, for the Reformed, acknowledges that “the Evangelists appear to differ,” offering the usual reconciliations. On “Behold your King,” Ellicott reads it as “bitter irony towards the Jews,” of a piece with the words over the cross (v.19).

The witnesses dwell on the double edge of Pilate’s taunt. He means to mock and to provoke — pointing to the beaten, thorn-crowned prisoner and sneering, “Behold your King,” as if to shame the nation for the figure they would crucify. Yet, as so often in this trial, the scorn carries a truth deeper than the scorner knows: this is indeed their King, the long-promised Son of David, standing in shame before them. The witnesses note the solemn timing — the preparation of the passover, when the lambs were being made ready for slaughter — so that the true Paschal Lamb is presented to the people at the very hour they prepared the type. Whatever the precise reckoning of the hour, the providence is plain: as Israel made ready to kill the passover, the Lamb of God was led forth to be slain. Pilate’s mocking “Behold your King” thus unwittingly announces the King who reigns from the cross, presented to his people in the hour of sacrifice.

The witnesses note that John alone fixes the scene at the preparation of the passover, and that this is no idle detail. The Evangelist sets the condemnation of Christ against the backdrop of the slain lambs, so that the reader cannot miss the meaning: the true Lamb is being led to the slaughter while Israel prepares the shadow. Whatever the difficulties of the hour, the theological note is unmistakable, and the church has heard in it the announcement that the substance of every passover stood before the people, presented as their King in the very hour of sacrifice.
15:
“But they cried out, Away with him, away with him, crucify him. Pilate saith unto them, Shall I crucify your King? The chief priests answered, We have no king but Caesar.” The witnesses dwell on the frenzied cry and the rulers’ astonishing renunciation. The grammatical-historical commentators note the passionate repetition: Robertson reads “Away with him, away with him” (aron, aron) as a cry that “has gotten on the nerves of the crowd,” and Clarke takes “Away with him” to mean “kill him” (cf. Isaiah 57:1). Pilate’s “Shall I crucify your King?” keeps up his taunt — “your King” standing emphatically first (Ellicott). The chief priests’ answer is the climax of their apostasy: “We have no king but Caesar.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, calls this “a display of shocking madness”: the priests, who “ought to have been well acquainted with the Law,” reject Christ, “in whom the salvation of the people was wholly contained, on whom all the promises depended,” and by rejecting him “deprive themselves” of their own hope. The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem bids us “look with awe” at the Lord who, having redeemed this people from Egypt, is now shouted against by them. The witnesses thus find in the verse the depth of the nation’s self-condemnation. To be rid of Christ, the rulers disown the very hope of Israel: a people who looked for Messiah their King declare they have no king but the heathen Caesar. In renouncing Christ they renounce their calling, casting away the promises and submitting to the Gentile yoke they so resented. The witnesses note the tragic irony — that the keepers of the Law, on the day of the passover, reject the King the Law foretold, and choose Caesar over Christ; and in that choice the old covenant nation pronounces, out of its own mouth, the sentence of its rejection.

The witnesses dwell on the finality of the words ‘We have no king but Caesar.’ Out of their hatred of Christ, the rulers say what no faithful Israelite could ever say, abjuring the throne of David and the hope of Messiah for the sake of Roman favour. The verse thus marks the nation’s formal repudiation of its calling, spoken by its own priests; and the church has long seen in it the self-pronounced sentence of a people who, to be rid of their King, embraced the very yoke they hated, and in rejecting Christ rejected the God who gave the promises.
16:
“Then delivered he him therefore unto them to be crucified. And they took Jesus, and led him away.” The witnesses mark the final surrender of Pilate and the irony of his act. The grammatical-historical commentators note that Pilate, finding he could not move the people, “passed sentence on him, and gave him up to their will” (Gill); Robertson observes the verb “delivered” (paredōken) — the same word used of the Sanhedrin handing Jesus to Pilate (John 18:30) — so that “now Pilate hands Jesus back… with full consent for his death” (cf. Luke 23:25). Clarke recalls the detail John omits, that Pilate first “washed his hands” (Matthew 27:24) to protest his innocence. Ellicott and Robertson note that the crucifixion was carried out by the Roman soldiers, though “delivered… unto them,” the chief priests, at whose direction it was done.

The Eastern Chrysostom reflects on the rulers’ frenzy, and the Latin Augustine notes that it was the soldiers, the governor’s guards, who led him away. Henry, for the Puritan expositors, sums the inward drama: Pilate “had a mighty struggle between his convictions and his corruptions; but at length his convictions yielded, and his corruptions prevailed, the fear of man having a greater power over him than the fear of God.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the tragic end of Pilate’s resistance. Convinced of Christ’s innocence, washing his hands of the deed, protesting to the last, he yet delivers the guiltless to death — the clearest proof that a divided conscience without courage will, in the end, do the very evil it deplores. The hand-washing cannot cleanse him; the responsibility he disowns is his. And yet, the witnesses note, behind the weakness of the judge stands the purpose of God: the Son is “delivered” up — by the Sanhedrin, by Pilate, and, deepest of all, by the Father (Romans 8:32) — that he might be led away to the cross for the world’s redemption.

The witnesses underline that the guilt is shared yet not therefore lessened. Pilate, the priests, the soldiers, Judas — each had his part, and each is answerable; the providence that overruled their deeds excuses none of them. Yet over all stands the deeper truth that the Son was delivered up by the Father’s set purpose and foreknowledge. The verse thus holds together, as the whole Passion does, the wickedness of men and the will of God: the same act that is the blackest crime of history is the appointed means of the world’s redemption.
17:
“And he bearing his cross went forth into a place called the place of a skull, which is called in the Hebrew Golgotha.” The witnesses note the burden Christ carried and the place of his death. The grammatical-historical commentators explain that condemned men were made to carry their own cross — or, Wesley specifies, “the transverse beam… to which his hands were afterward fastened.” Robertson reads “bearing the cross for himself,” after the scourging; Clarke notes that Jesus “bore it all alone first,” until, weakened by his usage, Simon of Cyrene was compelled to help (cf. Matthew 27:32). The place was “Golgotha,” the place of a skull — Calvary. The witnesses note that he “went forth,” outside the city gate.

Augustine, for the Latin fathers, ponders the meaning of the Lord bearing his own cross; Calvin, for the Reformed, draws the typology: “to prove that he is the sacrifice for our sins, he wished both to be led out of the city, and to be hanged” — for the sin-offering was burned outside the camp, and “Jesus also… suffered without the gate” (Hebrews 13:12; cf. Numbers 15:36). The witnesses thus find in the verse both the reality of Christ’s suffering and its sacrificial meaning. He carries the instrument of his own death — a picture, the church has long seen, of the believer who must take up his cross and follow. And he is led outside the city, in fulfilment of the law of the sin-offering, that as the bodies of the sacrifices were burned without the camp, so the great Sacrifice should suffer without the gate, bearing the reproach for his people. The lonely road to Golgotha, the borne cross, the place of the skull — all, the witnesses note, are at once sober history and rich with the meaning of the atonement: the Lamb led out to be slain for the sins of the world.

The witnesses find in the borne cross a pattern for every disciple. The Lord who said his followers must take up their cross and come after him goes before them, bearing his own to the place of death. So the verse joins the example of Christ to the doctrine of his sacrifice: he is at once the Saviour who suffers without the gate for our sins and the Master whose path of self-denial his people are called to tread. The lonely road to Golgotha is thus both the way of atonement and the way of discipleship.
18:
“Where they crucified him, and two other with him, on either side one, and Jesus in the midst.” The witnesses record the central fact of the Passion and the company in which Christ died. The grammatical-historical commentators note that the crucifixion was carried out by the Roman soldiers (Robertson), at Golgotha (Gill), with “two other” — robbers (lēistai, not mere thieves, Robertson; cf. Matthew 27:38), perhaps of the gang of Barabbas (Clarke) — one on each side, “and Jesus in the midst.” The placing, the commentators observe, was deliberate: Jesus is set in the central place, as the chief malefactor.

Calvin, for the Reformed, draws out the indignity and its meaning: “as if the severity of the punishment had not been sufficient,” Christ “is hanged between two robbers, as if he… had been the most wicked and the most detestable of them all.” Yet, he adds, “the wicked executioners of Christ did nothing but what God had decreed,” fulfilling the prophecy that “he was numbered with the transgressors” (cf. Isaiah 53:12). The witnesses thus find in the verse the lowest depth of Christ’s humiliation and, within it, a hidden glory. To be crucified at all was shame; to be crucified between two robbers, in the place of greatest dishonour, was to be reckoned the worst of criminals. Yet this very degradation fulfils the Scripture and displays the heart of the gospel: the sinless One numbered with transgressors, taking the central place of the condemned, that he might bear the condemnation of sinners. The witnesses note that even his position on the cross preaches — set in the midst, between the guilty, as the One who came to stand in the sinner’s place and to draw all men unto himself.

The witnesses observe how the placing of Christ between the two robbers preaches the gospel in a tableau. He hangs where the worst offender would hang, in the midst, that sinners on either hand — and all who look — might see the Substitute who took the place of the guilty. The verse thus turns even the malice of the executioners to the display of grace: numbered with transgressors, the sinless Lord is set among them as their fellow in death, that they might become his fellows in life. The cross of shame becomes the throne of mercy.
19:
“And Pilate wrote a title, and put it on the cross. And the writing was, JESUS OF NAZARETH THE KING OF THE JEWS.” The witnesses dwell on the inscription and its providential testimony. The grammatical-historical commentators explain the “title” (titlos, Robertson notes the technical Latin word, used only by John): the board naming the criminal and his crime, fixed to the cross (Gill; cf. Matthew 27:37; Mark 15:26). Robertson notes that John alone tells us “Pilate himself wrote it.” Wesley takes John’s wording as “undoubtedly… the very words,” the other Evangelists abridging. The charge, as Pilate framed it, was that this was “the King of the Jews.”

The Eastern Chrysostom finds a deeper meaning: “as letters are inscribed on a trophy declaring the victory, so Pilate wrote a title on Christ’s cross,” at once “distinguishing Christ from the thieves” and “exposing the malice of the Jews in rising up against their King.” Calvin, for the Reformed, marks the “extraordinary circumstance” that, while such titles named a crime, this one proclaimed a truth. The witnesses thus find in the title an unintended proclamation of Christ’s kingship. Pilate meant it as a final taunt at the Jews — see the King you crucify — and perhaps as a charge of treason; but God overruled the heathen’s pen to publish, over the head of the dying Saviour, the truth of who he is. The cross, meant to be the place of his deepest shame, bears the announcement of his royalty; the very instrument of his death is turned into a trophy of his kingship. What Pilate wrote in scorn, the witnesses read as a herald’s decree: Jesus of Nazareth, the King — reigning, as the title testifies, from the tree.

The witnesses note the firmness with which Pilate, having yielded everything else, holds to his title. The man who lacked courage to save Christ will not be moved to alter the inscription, so that, against his own weakness, he becomes the instrument of a true confession. The verse thus shows how God can make even a faltering and faithless judge publish the truth: over the head of the dying Saviour stands the unretracted decree that he is the King, written by the very hand that condemned him.
20:
“This title then read many of the Jews: for the place where Jesus was crucified was nigh to the city: and it was written in Hebrew, and Greek, and Latin.” The witnesses note the wide reading of the title and the three tongues in which it was written. The grammatical-historical commentators observe that the place of crucifixion, “nigh to the city,” drew crowds, so that “many of the Jews” read the inscription (Gill). The three languages, Clarke and the others note, were the great tongues of the ancient world — Hebrew (the sacred language of religion; Lightfoot specifies the Aramaic of the Jews), Greek (the language of culture), and Latin (the language of Roman power) — so that the charge could be read by all (cf. Luke 23:38). Ellicott marks this detail as “peculiar to St. John,” another instance of “his exact knowledge of what took place at Jerusalem.”

The witnesses find in the threefold inscription a fitting, if unintended, symbol. The title proclaiming Christ’s kingship was written in the three representative languages of mankind — the tongue of revelation, the tongue of wisdom, and the tongue of empire — so that Jew and Greek and Roman alike might read, over the cross, that Jesus is King. The witnesses see here a quiet foreshadowing of the gospel’s universal reach: the kingship first published on the cross in Hebrew, Greek, and Latin would be proclaimed to all nations in every tongue. What Pilate intended as a public notice of a crime became, by God’s overruling, the first multilingual herald of the King; and the place of shame, near the city where all could see, became a pulpit from which the truth of Christ was read by the whole world that passed by. The witnesses note the providence: the cross is set near the city, the title written for all to read, that the crucified King might be proclaimed to every kind of man.

The witnesses draw the wider lesson of the three tongues. The kingship of Christ was not to be a secret thing whispered among a few, but a proclamation for the whole world to read. That it was first published over the cross, in the languages of every people then known, foreshadows the day when every tongue shall confess that Jesus Christ is Lord. The verse thus turns the place of execution into a herald’s stand, from which the crucified King is announced, in the speech of religion, wisdom, and power alike, to all who pass by.
21:
“Then said the chief priests of the Jews to Pilate, Write not, The King of the Jews; but that he said, I am King of the Jews.” The witnesses note the rulers’ alarm at the wording and their attempt to alter it. The grammatical-historical commentators explain their motive: since the title could be read by all (v.20), the Messianic words “King of the Jews” seemed to honour the crucified and to disgrace the nation (Ellicott). Robertson catches the irony precisely: “the chief priests were uneasy for fear that the joke in the mock title was on them instead of on Jesus. They were right in their fear.” They ask that the inscription be changed to read merely that he claimed the title — “that he said, I am King of the Jews” — throwing the assertion onto Christ alone.

Calvin, for the Reformed, reads their request as the sting of a sharp rebuke: “they feel that they are sharply rebuked, and therefore would wish it changed, so as not to involve the nation in disgrace, but to throw the whole blame on Christ.” Yet in their very protest “they do not conceal their deep hatred of the truth, since the smallest spark of it is more than they are able to endure.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the rulers’ unwitting confession. They object to the title because it states a truth they cannot bear — that this crucified man is their King. Their demand to soften it to a mere claim only betrays how the truth galls them; the spark of it, even on a placard over a dying man, is intolerable. The witnesses note the same nation that had just cried “We have no king but Caesar” (v.15) now squirms under an inscription naming the King they disowned — caught, as Robertson says, in the joke that was on them, and unable to escape the testimony nailed above the cross.

The witnesses observe how the rulers, having gained the death they sought, cannot rest even in their victory. The title galls them more than the cross pleases them, for it publishes the very claim they crucified him for making. Their petty quarrel over the wording betrays a conscience uneasy beneath its triumph; and the church has long read in their discomfort a sign that the truth they meant to bury will not stay buried, but proclaims itself even over the place of death.
22:
“Pilate answered, What I have written I have written.” The witnesses dwell on the governor’s sudden, surprising firmness. The grammatical-historical commentators note the curt finality: Robertson reads the perfect tenses (ho gegrapha gegrapha) “with emphasis on the permanence of the accusation,” a “sudden spirit of stubbornness… to the surprise of the chief priests.” Barnes hears in it impatience — Pilate, “weary of their solicitations,” having “yielded contrary to the convictions of his own conscience,” now “declared his purpose to yield no farther.” Clarke notes that “the Roman laws forbad the sentence to be altered when once pronounced,” and Lightfoot that the form of words was a common rabbinic idiom of settled fact.

Calvin, for the Reformed, lifts the scene to providence: “Pilate’s firmness must be ascribed to the providence of God,” for the priests “attempted in various ways to change his resolution.” “Let us know, therefore, that he was held by a Divine hand, so that he remained unmoved.” The witnesses thus find in Pilate’s obstinacy a strange instrument of God. The man who lacked the courage to do justice and free the innocent now, in a small matter of wording, proves immovable — and by that very stubbornness publishes and preserves the truth of Christ’s kingship over his cross. The witnesses note the irony and the providence together: in the one thing where Pilate stands firm, he stands firm for the truth, refusing to unsay the testimony his own hand had written. God overrules even the weakness and willfulness of the judge, so that the title proclaiming Jesus King is fixed beyond recall — what was written stands written, a heathen’s unwitting confession that the Crucified is indeed the King.

The witnesses note that Pilate, who would not stand firm for justice, stands firm for a sentence — and so, against his intention, for the truth. The verse leaves him a figure of tragedy and of providence at once: too weak to save the innocent, yet made the unwilling keeper of the testimony to his kingship. The church has heard in his obstinate words an unconscious ‘Amen’ to the gospel: what God has written of his Son stands written, and no enemy can unsay it.
23:
“Then the soldiers, when they had crucified Jesus, took his garments, and made four parts, to every soldier a part; and also his coat: now the coat was without seam, woven from the top throughout.” The witnesses note the soldiers’ division of the spoil and the seamless robe. The grammatical-historical commentators explain that the executioners — a “quaternion” of four soldiers (Robertson; cf. Acts 12:4) — took the criminal’s clothes as their perquisite, dividing the outer garments into four. The “coat” (the inner tunic), being “without seam, woven from the top throughout,” could not be torn without ruin, and so was treated apart (Barnes, Ellicott). The witnesses note that John’s account is fuller than the others.

The Latin Augustine and others find meaning in the seamless coat. Calvin, for the Reformed, while warning against fanciful allegory, joins the church in seeing the indignity: the soldiers gamble at the foot of the cross while the Saviour dies above them, fulfilling the Scripture. Many of the fathers have read the seamless robe, woven whole from the top, as an emblem of the unity of the church or of the wholeness of Christ’s righteousness, not to be rent. The witnesses thus find in the verse both stark history and quiet symbol. The history is the callousness of the soldiers, who, having done their brutal work, sit down to divide a dying man’s clothes — the last poverty of Christ, stripped even of his garments. The symbol, drawn by the fathers, is the seamless robe: as Christ’s coat was not torn, so, many have said, the church he died to gather is to be kept one and undivided. Whether or not the symbol be pressed, the witnesses agree on the historical exactness — the parted garments, the seamless coat, the gambling soldiers — all recorded by an eyewitness and all, as the next verse shows, fulfilling prophecy.

The witnesses add that the stripping of Christ deepens the picture of his poverty and shame. He who clothes the lilies hangs naked before the world, his last possessions diced away beneath him. Yet the church has found comfort even here: the Lord was stripped that his people might be clothed in his righteousness, and the seamless robe, kept whole, has been taken as a token of that perfect covering which is not torn nor divided, but received entire by all who trust in him.
24:
“They said therefore among themselves, Let us not rend it, but cast lots for it, whose it shall be: that the scripture might be fulfilled, which saith, They parted my raiment among them, and for my vesture they did cast lots.” The witnesses note that the soldiers’ careless act fulfilled prophecy to the letter. The grammatical-historical commentators identify the quotation as Psalm 22:18, “closely following the Greek” (Ellicott). The two clauses are exactly distinguished in the event: they “parted” the outer garments “among them,” but for the seamless “vesture” they “cast lots” — Robertson notes the soldiers’ words, “let us not rend it… cast lots,” it being “too valuable to ruin.” Wesley marks that “no circumstance of David’s life bore any resemblance to this,” so that the psalmist, “personating the Messiah, spoke bare prophecy.”

Augustine, for the Latin fathers, dwells on the precision of the fulfilment, and Cyril of Jerusalem appeals to it as an “exact sign” of the foretold Passion. The witnesses thus find in the verse a striking proof of the prophetic Scriptures. Centuries before, the psalmist had described, in detail he could not have drawn from his own life, exactly what the soldiers would do — parting some garments, casting lots for one. The very heedlessness of the executioners, who knew nothing of the prophecy, became its unwitting fulfilment, the distinction between “parting” and “casting lots” answering precisely to the seamless coat. The witnesses note the lesson the Evangelist presses: that the cross, far from being the defeat of Christ, is the fulfilment of the Scriptures, every detail foreseen and foretold. Even the gambling of pagan soldiers over a dying man’s clothes was written long before in the Psalm of the suffering Messiah, so that the believer may see, in the smallest circumstance of the Passion, the hand of God carrying out his determined purpose.

The witnesses press the apologetic force of the fulfilment. An impostor could not have arranged the careless choices of a pagan execution-squad; yet the soldiers, dividing some garments and gambling for one, did precisely what the Psalm foretold. The verse thus stands among the many proofs that the Passion was no accident but the unfolding of a plan written ages before, so that the believer’s faith rests not on probability but on the sure word of prophecy, fulfilled to the letter at the cross.
25:
“Now there stood by the cross of Jesus his mother, and his mother’s sister, Mary the wife of Cleophas, and Mary Magdalene.” The witnesses contrast the faithful women at the cross with the gambling soldiers. The grammatical-historical commentators note the group standing near enough “not only to see him, but to hear him speak” (Gill); Robertson marks the “vivid contrast this to the rude gambling of the soldiers.” There is debate over the number and identity — whether three women or four are named — and over “Mary the wife of Cleophas,” usually identified with Alphaeus and so the mother of James the Less (Clarke, Ellicott; cf. Matthew 27:56). All note the presence, above all, of Jesus’ own mother, fulfilling Simeon’s word that a sword should pierce her soul (Luke 2:35).

Calvin, for the Reformed, draws the lesson of the women’s devotion and of Christ’s mind: even in his agony he “did not fail to perform the duty which he owed, as a son.” The witnesses thus find in the verse a scene of tender faithfulness amid the horror. While the soldiers gamble and the rulers mock, a little company of women — and the beloved disciple — stand by the cross in love and grief, unwilling to leave their Lord. Foremost is Mary his mother, watching her Son die, the prophesied sword piercing her own heart. The witnesses note the courage of these women, who braved the shame and danger to be near him when the apostles had fled; and they prepare the way for the next verses, in which the dying Saviour, seeing his mother and his beloved disciple together, will turn from his own anguish to provide for her who bore him. The faithful at the foot of the cross stand as a rebuke to the world’s cruelty and a pattern of the love that cleaves to Christ in his suffering.

The witnesses note the quiet courage that this scene records. To stand by the cross of a condemned man was to share his shame and risk the crowd’s hostility, yet these women would not leave him. Their faithfulness, when the boldest of the apostles had fled, rebukes every fear that keeps us at a safe distance from a suffering Lord, and shows that love, more than valour, holds fast in the dark hour. The church honours them as the first and truest mourners at Calvary.
26:
“When Jesus therefore saw his mother, and the disciple standing by, whom he loved, he saith unto his mother, Woman, behold thy son!” The witnesses dwell on the Saviour’s tender provision for his mother even from the cross. The grammatical-historical commentators stress that the address “Woman” conveys “no idea of disrespect” (Barnes; cf. John 2:4), being a title of honour, and that “Behold thy son” refers not to Jesus but to John — “behold, my beloved disciple shall be to you a son, and provide for you, and discharge toward you the duties of an affectionate child” (Barnes). Robertson marks the picture: “the dying Saviour thinks of the comfort of his mother,” the beloved disciple (whom “John is just modest enough not to name”) standing by. Lightfoot notes the practical care: Joseph being dead and Jesus now dying, his mother needed a protector.

Calvin, for the Reformed, paraphrases the meaning: “Henceforth I shall not be an inhabitant of the earth, so as to discharge to thee the duties of a son; therefore I put this man in my room, that he may perform my office,” giving her “one who will take as much care of you as if he had been your son.” The witnesses thus find in the word a marvel of filial love in the midst of redeeming agony. Bearing the sin of the world, in unspeakable suffering, Christ yet has thought for the earthly mother who bore him, and will not leave her unprovided. He commits her to the disciple he loved, giving her a son in his place. The witnesses note that this care, far from distracting from his great work, adorns it: the same Lord who as Saviour bears the curse for the world fulfils as a man the fifth commandment to the last, honouring his mother with his dying breath. In “Woman, behold thy son,” the witnesses hear both the tenderness of the Son and a quiet pattern of the duty of children to parents, sealed at the cross.

The witnesses marvel that, amid the agony of bearing the world’s sin, Christ’s thought turns to a single grieving woman. The greatness of his redeeming work did not crowd out the small duties of love; he who carried the sins of many still carried the care of his mother. The verse thus teaches that no burden, however vast, excuses the neglect of those near to us, and shows the heart of the Saviour to be as tender in its human affections as it is mighty in its divine compassion.
27:
“Then saith he to the disciple, Behold thy mother! And from that hour that disciple took her unto his own home.” The witnesses note the completion of the tender committal and the disciple’s prompt obedience. The grammatical-historical commentators read the double charge: as Mary is given a son, so John is given a mother — “one who is to be to thee as a mother” (Barnes). Ellicott marks the mutuality: “the loving heart of the disciple should find, as well as give, sympathy and support,” their “common loss” drawing them together. Barnes notes that John “seems to have been in better circumstances” and able to provide for her, and that “from that hour” — at once — he took her into his own home and care.

The witnesses dwell on the beauty and the example of the scene. Christ entrusts the two he loved most on earth — his mother and his dearest disciple — to one another, that each might comfort the other in the loss of him. And John, with no word of question, obeys: “from that hour” he takes Mary as his own. The witnesses find here a model of the love that should bind the family of Christ — the readiness to take up the burdens of those whom the Lord commits to our care. They note, too, the silent honour done to Mary: she is provided for, but as a woman to be cared for, not as one to be worshipped; the disciple takes her into his home, not onto an altar. The witnesses thus read the verse as the gracious close of the cross’s domestic scene: the dying Saviour, having made provision for his mother, is now free to turn wholly to the finishing of his great work, knowing that she who bore him is in faithful and loving hands.

The witnesses find in the disciple’s instant obedience a model of true discipleship. He does not weigh the cost or delay; from that very hour he takes the Lord’s mother as his own. So the verse joins the tenderness of Christ to the obedience of the believer, and sets before the church the duty of caring for one another’s burdens as Christ commits them to us. What the dying Saviour entrusts, the loving disciple receives — and the bond formed at the cross becomes a household of faith.
28:
“After this, Jesus knowing that all things were now accomplished, that the scripture might be fulfilled, saith, I thirst.” The witnesses note that even Christ’s cry of thirst was conscious and prophetic. The grammatical-historical commentators stress his full awareness: Robertson reads the perfect “are now finished” (tetelestai), Jesus “fully conscious of the meaning of his atoning death” (cf. John 13:1), and notes the play on “accomplished” and “fulfilled.” The thirst was real — Clarke lists “the fatigue… the grief… the heat of the day, and the loss of blood” as its natural causes, and the “fullest proof” of his true humanity — yet it also fulfilled the Scripture, “in my thirst they gave me vinegar to drink” (Psalm 69:21), which Barnes and Clarke identify.

Augustine, for the Latin fathers, hears in “I thirst” more than the body’s craving — “as if to say, ye have not done all; give me yourselves,” a thirst for the salvation of those who gave him vinegar. The witnesses thus find in the word both the reality of Christ’s manhood and the perfection of his obedience to Scripture. He who is true God is also true man, and feels the raging thirst that crucifixion brought; he does not feign or suppress it, but owns it, “I thirst,” proving the genuineness of his suffering. Yet even here, at the edge of death, he acts “knowing that all things were now accomplished,” and that one Scripture remained to be fulfilled — so that his very thirst is offered in conscious obedience, that the word of the psalmist might be completed. The witnesses note the wonder: the Lord who gives living water (John 4:14) thirsts on the cross, that his people might never thirst again; and the church has long heard in his “I thirst” the deeper longing of his love for the souls he died to save.

The witnesses note how the word holds the two natures together. That Christ thirsts proves him truly man, sharing our frame and its sufferings; that he thirsts ‘knowing all things were accomplished’ shows him truly the Lord of the hour, ordering even his own anguish to the fulfilment of Scripture. The verse thus guards both truths the church confesses: a real incarnation that felt real pain, and a sovereign Saviour who reigned from the cross even in the extremity of his weakness.
29:
“Now there was set a vessel full of vinegar: and they filled a sponge with vinegar, and put it upon hyssop, and put it to his mouth.” The witnesses describe the giving of the sour wine and the means of it. The grammatical-historical commentators identify the “vinegar” (oxous) as the “tart small wine… the common drink of the Roman soldiers” (Clarke), set near for the soldiers’ own use (Gill, Ellicott). Robertson, marking the imperfect “was set” (ekeito) as the eyewitness’s notice, distinguishes this from the drugged wine mingled with myrrh and gall which Jesus had refused before crucifixion (Mark 15:23; Matthew 27:34): that stupefying draught he would not take, but this simple wine, to moisten his lips for the final cry, he received. They raised it on a sponge fixed to a stalk of hyssop — which, Calvin and Wesley note, “grows as large as small shrubs” in that country, long enough to reach his mouth.

The witnesses dwell on the contrast between the two cups. The drugged wine, offered to dull the pain, Christ refused: he would drink the cup of suffering to its dregs, with full and unclouded mind. But the plain sour wine, asked in his thirst and given here, he accepted — not to ease his agony but to fulfil the Scripture and to clear his voice for the great cry of victory to come. The witnesses note Lightfoot’s puzzle that vinegar, not the customary myrrh-wine, was at hand, and find in it the providence that even the soldiers’ ordinary refreshment served the prophetic word. So the verse records, with an eyewitness’s exactness, the last drink of the dying Saviour: the vinegar of Psalm 69:21, raised on hyssop — the very plant used to sprinkle the blood of the passover and of cleansing (Exodus 12:22) — held to the lips of the true Paschal Lamb as he finished his work.

The witnesses draw the lesson of the two cups for the believer’s comfort. The stupefying draught Christ refused, that he might bear the full weight of the cup the Father gave him with an undimmed mind; the simple wine he took, that he might cry aloud the word of finished redemption. So the verse shows a Saviour who would not be spared the least of his appointed sufferings, drinking our cup to the dregs that we might drink the cup of salvation. The hyssop that raised the wine recalls the blood of the passover, sprinkling the doorposts of the redeemed.
30:
“When Jesus therefore had received the vinegar, he said, It is finished: and he bowed his head, and gave up the ghost.” The witnesses receive this as the great cry of accomplished redemption and the voluntary surrender of his life. The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the one word “It is finished” (tetelestai, the same perfect as in v.28): Robertson calls it “a cry of victory in the hour of defeat,” like “I have overcome” (John 16:33); Ellicott refers it to “the work which God had given Him to do” (John 17:4). Clarke expands the sense: “I have executed the great designs of the Almighty — I have satisfied the demands of his justice — I have accomplished all that was written in the prophets… and now the way to the holy of holies is made manifest through my blood.” Robertson notes that Jesus “gave up his spirit” voluntarily, dying not as one overpowered but as one laying down his life.

Calvin, for the Reformed, and Barnes dwell on the fullness of the word: “the work long contemplated, long promised, long expected by prophets and saints, is done… and man is redeemed” (Barnes). The witnesses thus find in “It is finished” the summary of the gospel. It is not the groan of a victim but the shout of a victor: the work of redemption, planned from eternity and foretold through the ages, is now complete; nothing remains to be added, no further satisfaction to be made, the price paid in full. And the manner of his death seals it — “he bowed his head, and gave up the ghost,” yielding his spirit by his own act, the Shepherd laying down his life of himself (John 10:18). The witnesses note that the believer’s whole hope rests on this one word: because Christ has said “It is finished,” there is nothing left for the sinner to do but receive the finished work — the atonement accomplished, the law satisfied, the way into the holiest opened by his blood.

The witnesses press that ‘It is finished’ forbids every addition to the work of Christ. If redemption is finished, then nothing of our own can be joined to it as ground of acceptance; the sinner is shut up to receive a completed salvation by faith alone. The verse thus stands as the death-blow to all self-righteousness and the charter of the believer’s peace: the work is done, the debt discharged, the way into the holiest opened — and the dying cry of the Saviour is the gospel in a single word.
31:
“The Jews therefore, because it was the preparation, that the bodies should not remain upon the cross on the sabbath day (for that sabbath day was an high day), besought Pilate that their legs might be broken, and that they might be taken away.” The witnesses note the rulers’ scruple and the high day. The grammatical-historical commentators explain the concern: the law forbade bodies to “remain all night upon the tree” (Deuteronomy 21:22–23), and to leave them over the sabbath — especially this sabbath, “an high day,” coinciding with the passover feast — was defilement (Barnes, Ellicott). Robertson notes “the Preparation” (paraskeuē, Friday) and the “great day” (megalē); Lightfoot lists why it was high — sabbath, the feast, and the offering of the firstfruits sheaf. So they ask that the legs be broken (the crurifragium) to hasten death and allow removal.

The Eastern Chrysostom marks the hypocrisy: “the Jews, who strained at a gnat and swallowed a camel,” after “their audacious wickedness, reason scrupulously about the day” — and, as Theophylact adds, “how forcible is truth: their own devices it is that accomplish the fulfilment of prophecy.” Calvin, for the Reformed, finds the narrative tending “to the edification of our faith,” containing “a mystery of no ordinary value.” The witnesses thus find in the verse a portrait of a conscience scrupulous in trifles and seared in great things. The rulers, having just murdered the Son of God, are anxious lest a dead body defile their holy day; they swallow the camel of deicide and strain at the gnat of ceremonial impurity. Yet, the witnesses note, their very scruple becomes the instrument of prophecy: their request to break the legs sets in motion the events — the legs of Jesus left unbroken, the side pierced — by which two more Scriptures would be fulfilled. The superstition of his enemies is overruled to seal the witness to the Lamb.

The witnesses observe the irony that the enemies’ very religiousness served the purposes of God. Anxious to keep their feast undefiled, the rulers set in motion the events that fulfilled the Scriptures concerning the Lamb. The verse thus shows how the Lord turns even the hypocrisy of his foes to the confirmation of his word, so that their scruple over a corpse on the sabbath became the occasion of the unbroken bone and the pierced side. Nothing in the Passion fell outside the design of heaven.
32:
“Then came the soldiers, and brake the legs of the first, and of the other which was crucified with him.” The witnesses note the soldiers carrying out Pilate’s grant upon the two robbers. The grammatical-historical commentators clarify that these are the same quaternion (v.23) who, “having naturally fallen back from the crosses, are here represented as coming forward to complete their work” (Ellicott), not a fresh company. They break the legs of the two crucified with Jesus — Robertson marks the participle “crucified with him” (synstaurōthentos, the word Paul uses of being “crucified with Christ,” Galatians 2:19) — the heavy blow hastening death so the bodies might be taken down before the sabbath.

The Eastern Origen, dwelling on the passover typology that fills this chapter, sees in the scene the working-out of the law of the true Paschal Lamb. The witnesses thus find in the verse the providential setting for the sign that follows. The soldiers begin their grim task at the outer crosses, breaking the legs of the two robbers; and the reader, knowing the law that “a bone of him shall not be broken” (to be cited in v.36), waits to see what they will do when they come to Jesus in the midst. The breaking of the robbers’ legs, the witnesses note, both shows the ordinary course the soldiers followed and throws into relief the extraordinary exception about to be made of Christ. What the soldiers did to the others by routine, they would not do to him — not by design, but because they found him already dead — so that the Scripture concerning the Lamb whose bones are unbroken might be fulfilled in the very act of a Roman execution-squad.

The witnesses note that the soldiers acted without the least thought of prophecy, yet served it exactly. Breaking the legs of the two robbers was the routine of their grim trade; but it framed the moment in which the law of the Paschal Lamb would be honoured in the body of Christ. So the verse reminds the reader that the fulfilment of Scripture at the cross was wrought not by friends arranging the scene but by careless enemies following their custom — a providence the more striking for being wholly unintended by its agents.
33:
“But when they came to Jesus, and saw that he was dead already, they brake not his legs.” The witnesses note the providential exception made of Christ. The grammatical-historical commentators observe that the soldiers, coming to Jesus, found him “already dead” (ēdē tethnēkota, Robertson, who comments “Jesus died before the robbers, died of a broken heart”), and so did not break his legs. Barnes notes that his death was “doubtless hastened by the intense agony of the garden, and the peculiar sufferings endured as an atonement for sin.” Ellicott observes that the purpose of the leg-breaking (crurifragium) was to ensure death; finding Jesus dead, the soldiers had no need to apply it to him.

Calvin, for the Reformed, sees in this “a very extraordinary work of the providence of God.” Ungodly men might say it happened naturally that one died sooner than another; but the believer recognises the hand of God, ordering even the soldiers’ omission so that not a bone of the Lamb should be broken. The witnesses thus find in the verse the quiet sovereignty of God over the smallest details of the Passion. The same soldiers who broke the robbers’ legs by routine pass Jesus by — not out of pity, for they had shown none, but because they found him dead — and so, without knowing it, fulfil the ancient law of the passover lamb and the word of the Psalm. The witnesses note that Christ’s having died first, “before the robbers,” is itself part of the design: his death was no slow expiring forced by the breaking of bones, but a willing surrender already accomplished, leaving his body to be honoured by the very exception the law required. The providence of God guards the bones of his Son even on the cross.

The witnesses find comfort in the care God takes for the body of his Son. Even in death, when Christ could feel no further indignity, the Father guards him, so that the soldiers’ spear-blow is withheld and not a bone is broken. The verse thus assures the believer that the providence which ordered the great matters of redemption stooped also to the smallest, fulfilling the type of the passover lamb in the very omission of a routine cruelty. Christ dead is as much under the Father’s keeping as Christ living.
34:
“But one of the soldiers with a spear pierced his side, and forthwith came there out blood and water.” The witnesses dwell on the piercing and the mysterious flow of blood and water. The grammatical-historical commentators take it first as the proof of death: the spear-thrust into the side, and the issue of “blood and water,” show that Jesus was truly and certainly dead (Gill, Robertson) — many finding a physiological explanation in the rupture of the heart and the separation of the blood. Lightfoot notes a tradition that it was the right side. The Evangelist records it, as the next verses insist, as an eyewitness of unquestionable fact, and as the fulfilment of Scripture (v.37; Zechariah 12:10).

The witnesses, with the long tradition of the church, find in the blood and water a deep significance. Calvin, for the Reformed, and others read the two streams as emblems of the twofold grace that flows from Christ’s death — the blood of justification and atonement, the water of cleansing and sanctification; some see in them the two sacraments, the Supper and baptism, others the double cure of sin’s guilt and power (cf. 1 John 5:6). The witnesses thus find the verse both a sober witness and a fountain of doctrine. As witness, it certifies the reality of Christ’s death — no swoon but a true death, sealed by the spear and the separated blood and water — the foundation of the gospel’s claim that he died and rose. As doctrine, it has been heard as the gospel in a figure: from the pierced side of the dead Saviour flow the very streams of salvation, blood to wash away the guilt of sin and water to cleanse its defilement, so that the church has sung of being hidden in the riven side, and of the double cure drawn from the wounds of Christ. The witnesses note that the soldier’s cruel thrust opened, in the providence of God, the fountain set open “for sin and for uncleanness” (Zechariah 13:1).

The witnesses note that John dwells on this sign with peculiar earnestness, swearing to it in the next verse, because so much hangs upon it. The reality of Christ’s death is the hinge of the gospel; a Saviour who only swooned could not save. And the blood and water that flowed have become, in the church’s reading, the emblem of all he purchased — pardon and purity, the guilt of sin atoned and its stain washed away. The riven side of the dead Redeemer is thus both the proof of his death and the open fountain of the believer’s cleansing.
35:
“And he that saw it bare record, and his record is true: and he knoweth that he saith true, that ye might believe.” The witnesses take this as the Evangelist’s solemn personal oath to what he saw. The grammatical-historical commentators identify the witness as John himself, who “in his great modesty frequently conceals himself” and “speaks of himself in the third person” (Gill, Ellicott; cf. John 1:7). Robertson stresses the perfect “he that hath seen” (ho heōrakōs) — “John the Apostle was there and saw this fact (still sees it)” — and draws the doctrinal point: “this personal witness disproves the theory of the Docetic Gnostics that Jesus did not have a real human body.” Clarke notes that John “must have been pretty near the cross to distinguish between the blood and the water,” and that the words contain an appeal, almost an oath, to the truth of his testimony. The purpose is stated: “that ye might believe.”

The witnesses dwell on the weight John lays upon this sign. He does not merely report the piercing and the blood and water; he stops to swear to it, staking his known character for truth upon it (Barnes), “that ye may believe.” The witnesses find here both the foundation of faith and a guard against error. The foundation is eyewitness fact: the Christian faith rests not on legend but on what was seen by men who were there, and John writes as one who stood by the cross and beheld the death of his Lord.
