The guard is against every denial of the reality of Christ’s humanity and death — the Docetism that would make his body a phantom and his suffering a show. Against all such, John’s sworn testimony stands: he saw the spear go in, he saw the blood and water flow, and he knows that he speaks the truth. The witnesses note the pastoral aim woven through it all — that the reader, resting on this faithful witness, might believe that Jesus truly died, and so truly lives to save.

The witnesses note the earnestness with which John, usually so reticent, presses his own testimony here. He breaks his habitual self-effacement to stand forth as a sworn witness, because the death of Christ is the cornerstone of all he writes. The verse thus reminds the church that her faith is anchored in history attested by those who were present; and it invites every reader to weigh the credibility of the witness, whose only motive, as he protests, was that we might believe and so have life in the name of the One he saw die.
36:
“For these things were done, that the scripture should be fulfilled, A bone of him shall not be broken.” The witnesses find in the unbroken bones the fulfilment of the law of the passover lamb. The grammatical-historical commentators trace the citation: most refer it to the command concerning the paschal lamb, “neither shall ye break a bone thereof” (Exodus 12:46; cf. Numbers 9:12), though Lightfoot notes it may also touch Psalm 34:20, “he keepeth all his bones.” Barnes states the typology plainly: “John here regards the paschal lamb as an emblem of Christ; and as in the law it was commanded that a bone of that lamb should not be broken, so… it was ordered that a bone of the Saviour should not be broken.” Robertson notes the free quotation of Exodus 12:46.

Calvin, for the Reformed, presses the type: Moses “takes for granted that that lamb was a figure of the true and only sacrifice by which the Church was to be redeemed,” so that “Christ is our passover” (1 Corinthians 5:7). The witnesses thus find in the verse the meeting of providence and prophecy. The soldiers’ not breaking Christ’s legs (v.33) was, on the surface, a chance of his being already dead; but John reveals the design behind it — that the Scripture of the passover lamb might be fulfilled in him. Christ is the true Lamb, of whom the passover was but the shadow; and as not a bone of the type was to be broken, so not a bone of the antitype was broken, even amid the violence of crucifixion. The witnesses note how the whole chapter has been shadowed by the passover — the feast at hand, the lambs being slain — and here the type is sealed: the Lamb of God, whose blood redeems, is kept whole in death, that the figure given to Israel a millennium and a half before might find its perfect answer in him.

The witnesses dwell on the comfort the type affords. The passover lamb, kept whole, sheltered Israel from the destroyer; the true Lamb, his bones unbroken even in death, shelters his people from the wrath to come. The verse thus weaves the oldest ordinance of redemption into the newest, showing that the cross was no improvisation but the appointed fulfilment of a figure given at the Exodus. The believer, looking on the unbroken bones of his Saviour, sees the seal that this is indeed the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world.
37:
“And again another scripture saith, They shall look on him whom they pierced.” The witnesses find in the spear-thrust the fulfilment of a second prophecy. The grammatical-historical commentators identify the citation as Zechariah 12:10: Robertson notes John’s verb “pierced” (exekentēsan) as a correct rendering of the Hebrew (cf. Revelation 1:7), differing from the Septuagint. Lightfoot and Ellicott discuss the textual form — the prophet’s “they shall look upon me whom they pierced,” the “me” being the Lord who speaks. Barnes marvels at “the wonderful providence of God, that so many scriptures were fulfilled in his death,” and “without any such design on the part of the men engaged in these scenes.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, draws out the deity implied: the Evangelist quotes it “to show that Christ is that God who formerly complained, by Zechariah, that the Jews had pierced his heart” — for the One pierced in the prophecy is the Lord himself. Wesley reads the looking two ways: those whose sins occasioned his sufferings “shall either look upon him in this world with penitential sorrow, or with terror when he cometh in the clouds of heaven” (Revelation 1:7). The witnesses thus find in the verse both a proof of prophecy and a summons. As proof, it joins the unbroken bone (v.36): the same Passion that fulfilled the passover type fulfilled also the word of Zechariah, the soldier’s spear accomplishing, without knowing it, what the prophet foretold. As summons, it sets before every reader the pierced Saviour and the two ways of looking upon him — the penitent gaze of faith that mourns for sin and finds cleansing in his wounds, or the terrified gaze of the lost when he returns in glory. The witnesses note the wonder that the very God who was pierced is the One on whom all shall look, and bid the reader look now, in faith and sorrow, that he may not look then in dread.

The witnesses press the tenderness and the warning of the prophecy together. The pierced One is to be looked upon — and how a man looks decides his destiny. To look in faith, mourning for the sins that drove the spear, is to find in those very wounds the source of pardon; to refuse that look now is to meet his gaze hereafter in judgment. The verse thus turns the historical piercing into a personal summons, bidding every reader fix his eyes on the crucified Saviour while it is yet the day of grace.
38:
“And after this Joseph of Arimathaea, being a disciple of Jesus, but secretly for fear of the Jews, besought Pilate that he might take away the body of Jesus.” The witnesses dwell on the courage of the secret disciple now grown bold. The grammatical-historical commentators note that Joseph had been “a disciple… but secretly for fear of the Jews” (Robertson’s perfect kekrymmenos), one of the rulers who through cowardice had hidden his faith (cf. John 12:42–43); yet now, when even the chosen apostles had fled, he goes openly to Pilate to beg the body (Barnes; cf. Matthew 27:57). The Eastern Chrysostom suggests Joseph thought “the hatred of the Jews would be appeased by His crucifixion,” and so “went with confidence”; the Latin Bede notes that Arimathaea is Ramatha, the city of Samuel.

Calvin, for the Reformed, and Wesley dwell on the change wrought in these timid men. Wesley sums it memorably: in that extremity “Joseph was no longer afraid, Nicodemus no longer ashamed,” acknowledging Christ “when even his chosen disciples forsook him.” The witnesses thus find in the verse a moving reversal. The cross, which scattered the bold, emboldened the timid: Joseph, who had hidden his discipleship for fear, now risks the rulers’ wrath and his own standing to claim the body of a condemned and crucified man. The witnesses note the providence in it — the body of Jesus, which “otherwise would have been exposed to the lawless violence of the soldiers” (Calvin), is rescued for an honourable burial by a man of rank and means, fulfilling that he should be “with the rich in his death” (Isaiah 53:9). And they find in Joseph an encouragement to every secret believer: the hour of trial that shames the fearful may be the very hour in which God brings hidden faith into the open, so that the disciple who dared not own Christ in safety confesses him in danger.

The witnesses find in Joseph’s boldness a rebuke and an encouragement. A rebuke, that one who had hidden his faith so long should need the cross to draw it out; an encouragement, that God can and does bring timid disciples to open confession in the hour of greatest danger. The verse thus holds out hope to the fearful believer that his secret faith need not remain secret forever, and that the Lord may yet make him brave where the bold have failed, to honour Christ when it costs the most.
39:
“And there came also Nicodemus, which at the first came to Jesus by night, and brought a mixture of myrrh and aloes, about an hundred pound weight.” The witnesses note the second timid disciple and his lavish gift. The grammatical-historical commentators recall that Nicodemus is mentioned only by John (John 3:1–2; John 7:50), and that he “at the first came to Jesus by night,” fearful — Robertson notes him “now at last taking an open stand.” He brings “myrrh and aloes,” aromatic drugs “used to preserve bodies from putrefaction” (Clarke), and in great quantity, “about an hundred pound weight” — a princely amount, fit for a king’s burial. The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem, dwelling on the garden of the tomb, hears an echo of the Canticles — “I am come into my garden” — and of the curse rooted out where the true Vine was planted.

The witnesses dwell on the costly devotion of these two men. Calvin, for the Reformed, and the others find in the lavish spices a love that, however late in declaring itself, now spares no expense to honour the body of the Lord. The witnesses thus find in Nicodemus, with Joseph, a portrait of faith brought to the light. The man who once crept to Jesus under cover of night now comes openly, in the face of the rulers’ fury, bearing a hundred pounds of spices to embalm a crucified man. The witnesses note the irony and the grace: while the chosen twelve had fled, two members of the very council that condemned him tend his burial with royal honours. And they find in the abundance of myrrh and aloes a testimony of love — not the prudent measure of the timid, but the overflowing gift of hearts at last unafraid, which would lavish on the dead Christ what they had been too fearful to confess to the living. The night-disciple becomes, at the burial, an open mourner of his Lord.

The witnesses observe that the burial of Christ was attended, not by the apostles he had taught, but by two counsellors who had feared to own him. The reversal teaches that grace is sovereign in its timing, drawing out faith when and where it will. And the costly spices, far beyond what custom required, preach the lavishness of love set free from fear: what these men had withheld in caution they now poured out without measure, teaching the church that true devotion, once awakened, counts no gift too great for the Lord.
40:
“Then took they the body of Jesus, and wound it in linen clothes with the spices, as the manner of the Jews is to bury.” The witnesses describe the careful, honourable wrapping of the body. The grammatical-historical commentators note that Joseph and Nicodemus “took the body” and “wound it in linen clothes” (othoniois, fine linen strips, Robertson) together with the spices, “as the manner of the Jews is to bury” (cf. John 11:44; Acts 5:6) — the body swathed in linen bands with the aromatic myrrh and aloes laid in the folds. The verb “to bury” (entaphiazein, Robertson) describes the customary Jewish preparation of a corpse.

Calvin, for the Reformed, finds the providence and the meaning together: “when Christ had endured extreme ignominy on the cross, God determined that his burial should be honourable, that it might serve as a preparation for the glory of his resurrection.” The great cost “may be thought by some superfluous; but we ought to consider the purpose of God.” The witnesses thus find in the careful burial both a token of love and a divine ordering. The body that hung in shame on the cross, stripped and pierced, is now tenderly washed of its blood, wrapped in clean linen, and laid amid a wealth of spices by reverent hands. The witnesses note that this honourable burial answers the indignity of the cross and prepares for the wonder of the empty tomb: the very linen clothes and spices, so carefully applied, would soon bear silent witness to the resurrection, found lying in the tomb while the Lord was risen. What love did here in sorrow, God would use for the proof of his Son’s victory; the burial of the King was the prelude to his rising.

The witnesses note that every detail of the burial was ordered to attest the reality of Christ’s death and to prepare the proof of his rising. The linen and the spices, lovingly applied, would be found undisturbed when he rose, silent witnesses to a resurrection no theft could counterfeit. The verse thus turns an act of mourning into a preparation for joy, reminding the believer that the tender care lavished on the dead body of the Lord was, in the providence of God, laying the very evidence by which his victory over death would be made sure.
41:
“Now in the place where he was crucified there was a garden; and in the garden a new sepulchre, wherein was never man yet laid.” The witnesses note the garden and the fresh, unused tomb. The grammatical-historical commentators observe that John’s account makes the choice of the sepulchre depend on its nearness to the place of crucifixion (Ellicott; cf. John 18:1), while the earlier Gospels note that it was Joseph’s own (Matthew 27:60). It was “new” (kainon, fresh, Robertson), “wherein was never man yet laid” — a rock tomb hewn in the hillside. Clarke notes the ancient custom of family burying-places in gardens (cf. 2 Kings 21:18).

Calvin, for the Reformed, insists this “did not happen by accident, but by an undoubted providence of God.” The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem hears in the garden an echo of the Canticles and of paradise — the curse that came in a garden begins to be undone in a garden, where the true Vine is laid in the earth. The witnesses thus find in the new tomb a detail rich with meaning and design. That it was a fresh sepulchre, never used, guards the resurrection from cavil: when Christ rose, none could say it was some other body, for no other had ever lain there. That it was in a garden the church has long pondered — as in a garden man fell, so near a garden the second Adam was buried and would rise, beginning the new creation. The witnesses note the providence in every circumstance: the nearness of the tomb suited the haste of the hour, its newness suited the honour of the King and the proof of his rising, and its setting in a garden suited the deep symbolism of the gospel — the place of death becoming the place of life, where the Lord of the garden would walk again on the resurrection morning.

The witnesses linger on the fitness of the garden. Where the first man, in a garden, brought death by his disobedience, the second Man is laid in a garden to conquer death by his obedience; and from a garden he will rise to begin the new creation. The verse thus invites the church to read the place of burial as more than circumstance: it is a quiet sign that the curse is being reversed at its source, and that the tomb in the garden is the seed-plot from which the resurrection life of all his people will spring.
42:
“There laid they Jesus therefore because of the Jews’ preparation day; for the sepulchre was nigh at hand.” The witnesses note the haste of the burial and the providence that placed the tomb so near. The grammatical-historical commentators explain that the lateness of the hour — the “preparation day,” with the sabbath drawing on at sunset — pressed them to bury quickly, and the nearness of the new sepulchre made it the convenient place (Gill). The body must be laid to rest before the high sabbath began; the garden tomb, close to the cross, served the urgency of the moment.

Calvin, for the Reformed, gathers the meaning of the whole burial-narrative: it was related “for various reasons,” and “did not happen by accident, but by an undoubted providence of God,” that the body of Christ was buried — truly dead, truly laid in the tomb, with reverent witnesses to the place. The witnesses thus find in this closing verse the quiet, sober end of the Passion. The Lord of glory, who had no place to lay his head, is laid at last in a borrowed tomb, buried in haste before the falling of the sabbath, in a garden near the place of his shame. Yet the witnesses note that nothing here is accidental: the haste, the nearness, the new tomb, the noble mourners, the careful spices — all are ordered by God to certify that Christ truly died and was truly buried, and to set the stage for the third day. The chapter that opened with the scourge and the thorns closes with the sealing silence of the grave; but the believer reads it knowing what the mourners did not yet know — that this borrowed sepulchre, nigh at hand, would hold the Lord of life only until the morning of resurrection.

The witnesses close the chapter on the note of stillness before the dawn. The body is laid, the stone is set, the sabbath falls, and for a day the Lord of life rests in the grave. Yet the believer reads the scene with hope the mourners lacked, knowing that death could not hold him. The verse thus leaves the reader at the threshold of the third day, the Passion finished and the burial sure, waiting upon the morning when the borrowed tomb would be found empty and the word ‘It is finished’ would be crowned by ‘He is risen.’
